primary_diagnosis.name: Kawasaki disease (complete, acute phase) with suspected acute cardiac involvement (myocarditis / early coronary involvement)
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Acute febrile mucocutaneous lymph node syndrome

comorbidities.name: Suspected acute myocarditis / coronary artery involvement

pathophysiology_summary: Kawasaki disease is an acute, self-limited medium-vessel vasculitis of unknown trigger, predominantly affecting children under 5. An intense inflammatory response injures the vascular wall and is tropic for the coronary arteries, where it can cause myocarditis, valvulitis and — the feared sequela — coronary artery aneurysms, thrombosis and myocardial infarction. The diagnosis is clinical: fever >=5 days plus four of five principal features (bilateral non-exudative conjunctival injection; oral mucosal changes including cracked lips and strawberry tongue; polymorphous rash; extremity changes with erythema/oedema then later periungual desquamation; and cervical lymphadenopathy). This child meets the complete criteria. Disproportionate tachycardia, a new murmur and an acute chest-pain episode signal cardiac inflammation and mandate urgent hospital assessment. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) within the first 10 days, with aspirin, sharply reduces the coronary aneurysm rate — so timely recognition is the whole game.

differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged fever >5 days plus red eyes and cracked lips in a young child is the classic opening for Kawasaki disease.
Common cause of fever and rash in a preschooler, but rarely produces fever unrelenting beyond 5 days with the full mucocutaneous constellation.
Strawberry tongue and rash overlap, but scarlet fever does not cause non-exudative conjunctivitis or extremity oedema and usually responds to antibiotics.
Fever, rash and red eyes fit, but no cough/coryza/Koplik spots and the child is fully vaccinated — must be actively excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should hold a broad febrile-child differential but already register that a >5-day unrelenting fever with mucocutaneous change is not a benign viral pattern.

differential.evidence_basis: Fever >5 days plus non-exudative conjunctivitis, lip/tongue changes, polymorphous rash, hand/foot erythema-oedema and cervical lymphadenopathy meets the complete clinical criteria.
Non-exudative conjunctivitis and indurative extremity oedema are against scarlet fever.
Toxic-appearing febrile child with rash and mucosal change — overlaps and is time-critical; distinguished by shock physiology and source.
No cough/coryza/Koplik spots and fully vaccinated.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this point the pattern is strongly Kawasaki; the value of the AI Doctor is recognising the seriousness and escalating, not clinching the label remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute chest-clutching episode + disproportionate tachycardia + a new murmur signal myocarditis/coronary involvement layered on complete Kawasaki criteria.
The new murmur, tachycardia out of proportion to fever and chest pain point to cardiac inflammation requiring urgent assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen converts an urgent same-day problem into a time-critical emergency (possible acute cardiac event).

differential.diagnosis: Kawasaki disease (complete) with suspected acute cardiac involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Complete clinical criteria plus red-flag cardiac features; needs emergency hospital assessment, echocardiography and IVIG. Remote confirmation is impossible.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position depends on inpatient echocardiography and inflammatory markers, which cannot be obtained via telehealth.

red_flags.description: Fever >=5 days that is unrelenting and unresponsive to antipyretics in a young child
red_flags.clinical_significance: A fever persisting beyond 5 days is the cardinal criterion of Kawasaki disease and, more broadly, a marker of serious illness demanding assessment rather than continued home antipyretics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissal as a self-limiting viral illness delays IVIG past the window that protects the coronary arteries.

red_flags.description: Sudden chest-clutching episode with disproportionate tachycardia and a new murmur — suspected acute myocarditis / coronary involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute cardiac inflammation or coronary compromise in a child is immediately life-threatening and can decompensate rapidly.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition risks missing an evolving myocardial event and rapid cardiovascular collapse.

red_flags.description: Toxic, inconsolable, lethargic appearance in a 4-year-old
red_flags.clinical_significance: A floppy, inconsolable, toxic-appearing young child is a non-specific but powerful marker of serious illness requiring urgent in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to escalate a toxic-appearing child can miss sepsis, toxic shock or cardiac decompensation.

red_flags.description: Mucocutaneous constellation — non-exudative conjunctivitis, cracked lips/strawberry tongue, polymorphous rash and erythematous indurative oedema of hands/feet
red_flags.clinical_significance: Together with prolonged fever this constellation is the Kawasaki fingerprint and separates it from benign viral rashes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'a viral rash' misses a treatable vasculitis with a narrow therapeutic window.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating fever + rash in a preschooler as a self-limiting virus and advising home antipyretics, ignoring that the fever has already run beyond 5 days with a full mucocutaneous constellation.
Latching onto strawberry tongue and rash to call scarlet fever, prescribing antibiotics and sending home — missing the non-exudative conjunctivitis and extremity oedema that point to Kawasaki.
Focusing on the rash/fever and not recognising that the acute chest episode, disproportionate tachycardia and new murmur have escalated this to a possible acute cardiac emergency.
Attempting to confirm or exclude Kawasaki remotely (it needs echocardiography and bloods) instead of escalating; the telehealth-correct action is recognise-and-escalate.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Fever >=5 days plus 5/5 principal features present — complete Kawasaki disease
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Complete criteria met; urgent IVIG plus aspirin indicated within 10 days of onset to reduce coronary aneurysm risk.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate
Degree and character of cervical lymphadenopathy on palpation
True indurative oedema of the extremities on examination
Echocardiography for coronary artery aneurysms and ventricular function
Inflammatory markers and blood tests (FBC/platelets, CRP, ESR, LFTs, albumin)
12-lead ECG for evidence of myocardial ischaemia
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a critically unwell febrile child with a full mucocutaneous constellation and a possible acute cardiac event; call 000 for an emergency ambulance to a paediatric emergency department; do not give further over-the-counter medications; keep the child calm and comforted; begin paediatric CPR if he becomes unresponsive and stops breathing normally.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The danger is UNDER-action — reassuring the parents that it is a viral rash or scarlet fever and advising home care or oral antibiotics, thereby missing the time-critical window for hospital IV treatment that protects the heart.

guidelines_referenced.reference: AHA Scientific Statement on Diagnosis, Treatment and Long-Term Management of Kawasaki Disease (2017)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Complete Kawasaki disease = fever >=5 days plus >=4 of 5 principal clinical features; treat with IVIG and aspirin within the first 10 days to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RCH Melbourne Clinical Practice Guideline — Kawasaki disease
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspect Kawasaki disease in any child with fever >=5 days; arrange urgent paediatric assessment, echocardiography and IVIG; incomplete/atypical forms still warrant treatment.
